serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of butter fried frog legs weighs approximately 300 g and provides around 650 kcal, making it a protein-rich main course typically shared or consumed as a single serving in a Vietnamese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Frog legs, raw (FDC ID 174474) and Butter, salted (FDC ID 173410), combined with fish sauce and garlic component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for butter-fried frog legs as a prepared Vietnamese dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw frog legs (FDC ID 174474), salted butter (FDC ID 173410), fish sauce (FDC ID 173469), and garlic (FDC ID 169230), weighted to reflect a typical Vietnamese Ếch Chiên Bơ preparation with approximately 60% frog legs, 15% butter, 10% fish sauce, 10% garlic and aromatics, and 5% other seasonings, adjusted for frying fat absorption. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these USDA component estimates and found to be broadly consistent; USDA-derived estimates are used as the primary evidence basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed protein-fat dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for butter-fried frog legs. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogy with other low-carbohydrate, high-protein fried meat dishes (e.g., fried chicken, GI ~40; mixed protein-fat dishes, GI 30–45) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.0 g/100g, primarily from garlic and light coating, resulting in a low glycaemic impact. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 15.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (400 mg/100g) from fish sauce and elevated saturated fat (6.7 g/100g) from butter
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high sodium content of 400 mg per 100 g driven by fish sauce, and its saturated fat content of 6.7 g per 100 g from butter, both of which exceed recommended thresholds; if consumed, request reduced fish sauce, substitute butter with a small amount of extra virgin olive oil, and choose pan-frying over deep-frying.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.0 g/100g) and low sugar (1.7 g/100g) with high protein (15.0 g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has a low glycaemic impact with only 5.0 g carbohydrate and 1.7 g sugar per 100 g, and its high protein content of 15.0 g per 100 g supports stable postprandial blood glucose; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total sodium intake from fish sauce and request pan-frying to reduce caloric density from fat.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (6.7 g/100g), trans fat (0.17 g/100g), high sodium (400 mg/100g), and cholesterol (83 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its elevated saturated fat of 6.7 g per 100 g and trans fat of 0.17 g per 100 g from butter, combined with high sodium of 400 mg per 100 g and cholesterol of 83 mg per 100 g, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; substituting butter with extra virgin olive oil, reducing fish sauce, and choosing pan-frying over deep-frying would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat exceeds 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is unsuitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis because deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation, and saturated fat at 6.7 g per 100 g from butter exceeds the 5 g threshold; patients should request pan-frying or stir-frying and substitute butter with extra virgin olive oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Fried Frog Legs (Vietnamese: Ếch Chiên Bơ, romanized: Ech Chien Bo) is a Vietnamese dish consisting of frog legs marinated and fried in butter with garlic, fish sauce, black pepper, chilli, and spring onion. The preparation typically involves pan-frying or deep-frying the frog legs in butter until the exterior is golden, crispy, and aromatic, while the interior remains tender and moist. Frog legs have a mild, delicate flavour often compared to chicken, and the butter-garlic combination imparts a rich, savoury taste with a slightly spicy finish from chilli and black pepper. The dish is popular in Vietnamese cuisine and is served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 217 kcal, 15 g protein, 15 g fat (of which 6.7 g is saturated fat from butter), and only 5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce. The dish has a low glycaemic impact (estimated GI 35) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions, but is of high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat content. Deep-frying also generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, making it unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients. A standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 650 kcal and 45 g protein.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for butter-fried frog legs as a prepared Vietnamese dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central component data for raw frog legs, salted butter, fish sauce, and garlic, weighted to reflect a typical Ếch Chiên Bơ preparation. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with these USDA-derived estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (frog legs FDC 174474, butter FDC 173410, fish sauce FDC 173469, garlic FDC 169230) as no single approved-source entry exists for this prepared Vietnamese dish. Input ai_generated values were cross-checked and found broadly consistent. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific butter-fried frog leg preparation are not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8) are estimated from USDA butter and frog leg fatty acid profiles. 4. potassium_mg (220.0) is estimated from USDA frog legs data. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is marked false as frog is considered haram in mainstream Shafi'i Islamic jurisprudence followed in Singapore and Malaysia; consumers should verify with their religious authority. 7. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore under the name 'Butter Fried Frog (Ech Chien Bo)'; country_of_origin in the input is listed as Singapore reflecting the point of sale, but the dish's culinary origin is Vietnam.
